title: C. difficile Infection
oneLiner: Toxin-mediated diarrhoeal disease triggered by antibiotics; causes colitis, can progress to toxic megacolon and death if missed or mistreated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Toxic megacolon** – Single finding: abdominal distension + rebound tenderness or rigidity + dilated colon ≥6 cm on AXR (or >5.5 cm at caecum). Immediate surgical consult.
- **Colonic perforation** – Single finding: sudden severe abdominal pain with board-like rigidity and free air under diaphragm on erect CXR or CT. Requires emergency laparotomy.
- **Septic shock** – Single finding: NEWS2 ≥7 (or lactate >4 mmol/L) despite initial fluids. Needs ICU referral, central line, vasopressors. Do not delay transfer waiting for stool result.
sections.Presentation:
- Watery, foul-smelling diarrhoea (≥3 unformed stools in 24h), often after recent antibiotic use (especially clindamycin, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones)
- Abdominal cramping, tenderness, low-grade fever, malaise
- Key exam: generalised abdominal tenderness (not peritonism initially), decreased bowel sounds if ileus, signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, postural hypotension)
- In severe disease: elevated temperature >38.5°C, distended abdomen, rebound, or guarding (alarm for megacolon)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Antibiotic-associated diarrhoea without *C. diff*** – most common; diarrhoea stops when antibiotic stopped, no systemic signs, negative stool tests
- **Inflammatory bowel disease (UC/Crohn’s)** – bloody diarrhoea, chronic history, extra-intestinal manifestations (joints, skin, eyes). **Splitter:** perianal disease / fistulas point to Crohn’s, not *C. diff*.
- **Other infectious colitis** – *Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli* O157; ask about food history, travel, blood in stool. Stool culture differentiates.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – older patient, sudden onset, left-sided pain, rapid resolution, history of vascular disease; stool *C. diff* negative.
- **Laxative abuse** – history of eating disorder or constipation; stool tests negative, no fever.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** NEWS2, fluid balance chart, stool frequency chart (record Bristol stool type). For suspected toxic megacolon: AXR immediately.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC count – leucocytosis common), U&E, CRP, lactate. Send blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:** *AXR* – first line if abdominal distension (dilated colon, thumbprinting, or toxic megacolon). *CT abdomen with contrast* – second line if perforation suspected or diagnosis unclear (shows colonic wall thickening, pancolitis, or free air).
- **Special tests:**
  - *Stool test for *C. difficile*:*
    - **Two-step algorithm (NICE/ESCMID):** First test GDH (glutamate dehydrogenase) – highly sensitive. If positive, then test toxin A/B EIA. If GDH+ / toxin+, treat. If GDH+ / toxin-, do PCR (detects toxin gene) – treat only if PCR+ and clinical suspicion high (toxin-negative PCR+ may indicate colonisation, not active infection; local policy may vary).
  - Do NOT repeat stool test for test of cure – clinical response is sufficient.
- **Never-miss test:** Abdominal X-ray (erect or supine) in any patient with *C. diff* and abdominal distension, pain, or ileus – to rule out toxic megacolon before starting medical therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Infection control:** Single room with contact precautions (gown + gloves) – vital to prevent hospital outbreaks. Notify infection control team.
  - **IV fluids:** aggressive crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) for hypotension or signs of dehydration. Start at 30 mL/kg if in shock (NEWS2 ≥5).
  - **Stop causative antibiotics** if possible (discuss with team); if ongoing infection requires antibiotics, switch to narrower-spectrum agents with low *C. diff* risk (e.g., aminoglycosides, tigecycline, metronidazole itself).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Antibiotic therapy:** Start empiric therapy based on severity (see Drugs & Doses). Re-evaluate when stool results return.
  - **Monitor stool frequency, abdominal exam, NEWS2 hourly for first 24h if severe.**
  - **Avoid anti-motility agents** (loperamide, opiate constipating agents) – risk of precipitating toxic megacolon.
  - If severe/fulminant: involve surgical team early for possible colectomy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For first episode: treat for 10-14 days. Do not repeat stool test.
  - For first recurrence: fidaxomicin or vancomycin tapered/pulsed regimen (per local policy). Consider faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) after ≥2 recurrences.
  - Ensure good hand hygiene (soap and water, not alcohol gel – spores are alcohol-resistant).
sections.Default Orders (If Admitted):
- **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) at maintenance rate (e.g., 1 mL/kg/h) plus replacement of ongoing losses. Adjust for shock.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, unless contraindicated due to high bleeding risk).
- **Diet:** generally normal diet unless NBM for surgical review or ileus. Encourage oral intake.
- **Observations:** NEWS2 q4h (mild), q1-2h (severe). Stool chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (per NICE NG113):**
  - *Mild disease* (WCC <15, CRP <150, no toxic signs): **Oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 10 days** (or intravenous if oral not possible – but PO preferred).
  - *Severe disease* (WCC >15, CRP >150, rising creatinine, or signs of colitis): **Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days** (or oral fidaxomicin 200 mg twice daily for 10 days as alternative).
  - *Fulminant disease* (toxic megacolon, perforation, shock): **Oral vancomycin 500 mg four times daily + intravenous metronidazole 500 mg three times daily** (discuss with microbiologist); surgical consult.
- **Common alternatives:** Fidaxomicin for severe first episode or first recurrence (200 mg BD 10d). Metronidazole IV (500 mg TDS) if oral route not possible.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Metronidazole – avoid alcohol (disulfiram reaction), potent CYP inhibitor (warfarin interaction). Vancomycin – minimal systemic absorption, safe in renal impairment but adjust IV dosing if used (rare). Fidaxomicin – minimal systemic exposure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Metronidazole – reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (e.g., 400 mg BD). No adjustment for vancomycin PO. Fidaxomicin no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using IV metronidazole alone for severe disease** – insufficient colonic levels; must be combined with oral vancomycin. Also, **prescribing oral metronidazole in fulminant colitis** when patient is vomiting or has ileus – needs IV route or NG tube.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Toxic megacolon (colonic dilatation >6 cm with systemic toxicity), colonic perforation, hypovolaemic shock, renal failure, bowel obstruction, death.
- **Chronic:** Recurrent infection (20-30% after first episode), chronic diarrhoea, malnutrition, post-infectious IBS.
sections.Prognosis: - First episode: 85-90% respond to specific therapy. Severe disease mortality can reach 10-15%. Recurrence common: 20% after first, 40-60% after ≥2 recurrences. Overall attributable mortality ~2-5% in hospitalised patients.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get a stool sample for *C. diff* before starting empiric therapy** if possible (treatment delays are rarely catastrophic in mild cases, but false positives from testing colonisation can lead to unnecessary antibiotics).
- **If patient has diarrhoea while on laxatives, stop laxatives first** and document stool chart before sending *C. diff* test.
- **Notify infection control immediately** – one case can cause an outbreak. Use soap and water for hand hygiene (alcohol gel ineffective).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT treat positive *C. diff* test in a patient without diarrhoea** – this likely represents colonisation, not infection. Treating can precipitate *C. diff* diarrhoea and cause harm.
- **Do NOT repeat stool tests for test of cure** – clinical response is sufficient; persistent positive toxin after treatment is common and does not indicate need for further therapy unless symptoms recur.
- **Do NOT give anti-motility agents** (loperamide, codeine, opioids) for diarrhoea control – risk of toxic megacolon.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening diarrhoea (>10 stools/day), new abdominal pain or distension, fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids, or signs of dehydration (dizziness, dark urine).
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week to review stool chart and ensure symptoms resolving. If no improvement after 5-7 days of treatment, re-admit or refer to gastroenterology.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / medical registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5, WCC >25 or <2, lactate >2 mmol/L, rising creatinine (>1.5x baseline), new abdominal distension, or failure to improve after 48h of appropriate therapy.
- **Call ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluids, need for vasopressors, signs of toxic megacolon (dilated colon, peritonism), or need for urgent colectomy.
- **Crash call if:** cardiac arrest, severe hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg not responding to fluids), or sudden severe abdominal pain with peritonism (suspect perforation).

rawMarkdown:
## C. difficile Infection
**One-liner:** Toxin-mediated diarrhoeal disease triggered by antibiotics; causes colitis, can progress to toxic megacolon and death if missed or mistreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Toxic megacolon** – Single finding: abdominal distension + rebound tenderness or rigidity + dilated colon ≥6 cm on AXR (or >5.5 cm at caecum). Immediate surgical consult.
- **Colonic perforation** – Single finding: sudden severe abdominal pain with board-like rigidity and free air under diaphragm on erect CXR or CT. Requires emergency laparotomy.
- **Septic shock** – Single finding: NEWS2 ≥7 (or lactate >4 mmol/L) despite initial fluids. Needs ICU referral, central line, vasopressors. Do not delay transfer waiting for stool result.

### Presentation
- Watery, foul-smelling diarrhoea (≥3 unformed stools in 24h), often after recent antibiotic use (especially clindamycin, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones)
- Abdominal cramping, tenderness, low-grade fever, malaise
- Key exam: generalised abdominal tenderness (not peritonism initially), decreased bowel sounds if ileus, signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, postural hypotension)
- In severe disease: elevated temperature >38.5°C, distended abdomen, rebound, or guarding (alarm for megacolon)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Antibiotic-associated diarrhoea without *C. diff*** – most common; diarrhoea stops when antibiotic stopped, no systemic signs, negative stool tests
- **Inflammatory bowel disease (UC/Crohn’s)** – bloody diarrhoea, chronic history, extra-intestinal manifestations (joints, skin, eyes). **Splitter:** perianal disease / fistulas point to Crohn’s, not *C. diff*.
- **Other infectious colitis** – *Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli* O157; ask about food history, travel, blood in stool. Stool culture differentiates.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – older patient, sudden onset, left-sided pain, rapid resolution, history of vascular disease; stool *C. diff* negative.
- **Laxative abuse** – history of eating disorder or constipation; stool tests negative, no fever.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** NEWS2, fluid balance chart, stool frequency chart (record Bristol stool type). For suspected toxic megacolon: AXR immediately.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC count – leucocytosis common), U&E, CRP, lactate. Send blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:** *AXR* – first line if abdominal distension (dilated colon, thumbprinting, or toxic megacolon). *CT abdomen with contrast* – second line if perforation suspected or diagnosis unclear (shows colonic wall thickening, pancolitis, or free air).
- **Special tests:**
  - *Stool test for *C. difficile*:*
    - **Two-step algorithm (NICE/ESCMID):** First test GDH (glutamate dehydrogenase) – highly sensitive. If positive, then test toxin A/B EIA. If GDH+ / toxin+, treat. If GDH+ / toxin-, do PCR (detects toxin gene) – treat only if PCR+ and clinical suspicion high (toxin-negative PCR+ may indicate colonisation, not active infection; local policy may vary).
  - Do NOT repeat stool test for test of cure – clinical response is sufficient.
- **Never-miss test:** Abdominal X-ray (erect or supine) in any patient with *C. diff* and abdominal distension, pain, or ileus – to rule out toxic megacolon before starting medical therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Infection control:** Single room with contact precautions (gown + gloves) – vital to prevent hospital outbreaks. Notify infection control team.
  - **IV fluids:** aggressive crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) for hypotension or signs of dehydration. Start at 30 mL/kg if in shock (NEWS2 ≥5).
  - **Stop causative antibiotics** if possible (discuss with team); if ongoing infection requires antibiotics, switch to narrower-spectrum agents with low *C. diff* risk (e.g., aminoglycosides, tigecycline, metronidazole itself).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Antibiotic therapy:** Start empiric therapy based on severity (see Drugs & Doses). Re-evaluate when stool results return.
  - **Monitor stool frequency, abdominal exam, NEWS2 hourly for first 24h if severe.**
  - **Avoid anti-motility agents** (loperamide, opiate constipating agents) – risk of precipitating toxic megacolon.
  - If severe/fulminant: involve surgical team early for possible colectomy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For first episode: treat for 10-14 days. Do not repeat stool test.
  - For first recurrence: fidaxomicin or vancomycin tapered/pulsed regimen (per local policy). Consider faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) after ≥2 recurrences.
  - Ensure good hand hygiene (soap and water, not alcohol gel – spores are alcohol-resistant).

### Default Orders (If Admitted)
- **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) at maintenance rate (e.g., 1 mL/kg/h) plus replacement of ongoing losses. Adjust for shock.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, unless contraindicated due to high bleeding risk).
- **Diet:** generally normal diet unless NBM for surgical review or ileus. Encourage oral intake.
- **Observations:** NEWS2 q4h (mild), q1-2h (severe). Stool chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (per NICE NG113):**
  - *Mild disease* (WCC <15, CRP <150, no toxic signs): **Oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 10 days** (or intravenous if oral not possible – but PO preferred).
  - *Severe disease* (WCC >15, CRP >150, rising creatinine, or signs of colitis): **Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days** (or oral fidaxomicin 200 mg twice daily for 10 days as alternative).
  - *Fulminant disease* (toxic megacolon, perforation, shock): **Oral vancomycin 500 mg four times daily + intravenous metronidazole 500 mg three times daily** (discuss with microbiologist); surgical consult.
- **Common alternatives:** Fidaxomicin for severe first episode or first recurrence (200 mg BD 10d). Metronidazole IV (500 mg TDS) if oral route not possible.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Metronidazole – avoid alcohol (disulfiram reaction), potent CYP inhibitor (warfarin interaction). Vancomycin – minimal systemic absorption, safe in renal impairment but adjust IV dosing if used (rare). Fidaxomicin – minimal systemic exposure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Metronidazole – reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (e.g., 400 mg BD). No adjustment for vancomycin PO. Fidaxomicin no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Using IV metronidazole alone for severe disease** – insufficient colonic levels; must be combined with oral vancomycin. Also, **prescribing oral metronidazole in fulminant colitis** when patient is vomiting or has ileus – needs IV route or NG tube.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Toxic megacolon (colonic dilatation >6 cm with systemic toxicity), colonic perforation, hypovolaemic shock, renal failure, bowel obstruction, death.
- **Chronic:** Recurrent infection (20-30% after first episode), chronic diarrhoea, malnutrition, post-infectious IBS.

### Prognosis
- First episode: 85-90% respond to specific therapy. Severe disease mortality can reach 10-15%. Recurrence common: 20% after first, 40-60% after ≥2 recurrences. Overall attributable mortality ~2-5% in hospitalised patients.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get a stool sample for *C. diff* before starting empiric therapy** if possible (treatment delays are rarely catastrophic in mild cases, but false positives from testing colonisation can lead to unnecessary antibiotics).
- **If patient has diarrhoea while on laxatives, stop laxatives first** and document stool chart before sending *C. diff* test.
- **Notify infection control immediately** – one case can cause an outbreak. Use soap and water for hand hygiene (alcohol gel ineffective).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT treat positive *C. diff* test in a patient without diarrhoea** – this likely represents colonisation, not infection. Treating can precipitate *C. diff* diarrhoea and cause harm.
- **Do NOT repeat stool tests for test of cure** – clinical response is sufficient; persistent positive toxin after treatment is common and does not indicate need for further therapy unless symptoms recur.
- **Do NOT give anti-motility agents** (loperamide, codeine, opioids) for diarrhoea control – risk of toxic megacolon.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening diarrhoea (>10 stools/day), new abdominal pain or distension, fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids, or signs of dehydration (dizziness, dark urine).
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week to review stool chart and ensure symptoms resolving. If no improvement after 5-7 days of treatment, re-admit or refer to gastroenterology.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / medical registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5, WCC >25 or <2, lactate >2 mmol/L, rising creatinine (>1.5x baseline), new abdominal distension, or failure to improve after 48h of appropriate therapy.
- **Call ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluids, need for vasopressors, signs of toxic megacolon (dilated colon, peritonism), or need for urgent colectomy.
- **Crash call if:** cardiac arrest, severe hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg not responding to fluids), or sudden severe abdominal pain with peritonism (suspect perforation).
